{{One source|date=February 9, 2014 (Sun) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|ja|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）}}]] '''Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave''' (玉城朝薫の墓) is the tomb of the progenitor of {{ill|Kumiodori|ja|組踊}}, [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]], located in the {{ill|Maeda (Urasoe)|ja|前田 (浦添市)|lt=Maeda|lt_ja=前田}} area of {{ill|Urasoe City|ja|浦添市}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}. The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a {{ill|Historic Site|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|3=史跡}} by Urasoe City.
==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of {{ill|Kunigami Village|ja|国頭村}}). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful {{ill|Daimyō|ja|大名}} family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切}} (presently part of {{ill|Nanjo City|ja|南城市}} (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓}}. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a {{ill|Bachi|ja|撥}}, and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in {{ill|Shuri|ja|首里}} in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with {{ill|Hansen's Disease|ja|ハンセン病}}, so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the {{ill|bone washing|ja|洗骨}} took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓}} style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the {{ill|Gosamaru|ja|護佐丸}} grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦}}, with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.
==See Also== * [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]] * [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]]
==External Links== * [http://www.city.urasoe.lg.jp/article.php/siko00193 'I Want to Visit the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun' (Urasoe City Homepage)]

In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a {{ill|Bachi|ja|撥|12=draft}}, and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|12=draft}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|12=draft}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|12=draft}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|12=draft}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|12=draft}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|12=draft}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in {{ill|Shuri|ja|首里|12=draft}} in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with {{ill|Hansen's Disease|ja|ハンセン病|12=draft}}, so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the {{ill|bone washing|ja|洗骨|12=draft}} took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|12=draft}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|12=draft}} style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the {{ill|Gosamaru|ja|護佐丸|12=draft}} grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|12=draft}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦|12=draft}}, with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.
==See Also== * [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]] * [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]]
==External Links== * [http://www.city.urasoe.lg.jp/article.php/siko00193 'I Want to Visit the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun' (Urasoe City Homepage)]

{{One source|date=February 9, 2014 (Sun) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|ja|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）|12=draft|12=draft}}]] '''Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave''' (玉城朝薫の墓) is the tomb of the progenitor of {{ill|Kumiodori|ja|組踊|12=draft|12=draft}}, [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]], located in the {{ill|Maeda (Urasoe)|ja|前田 (浦添市)|lt=Maeda|lt_ja=前田|12=draft|12=draft}} area of {{ill|Urasoe City|ja|浦添市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|12=draft|12=draft}}. The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a {{ill|Historic Site|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|3=史跡|12=draft|12=draft}} by Urasoe City.
==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内|12=draft|12=draft}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切|12=draft|12=draft}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of {{ill|Kunigami Village|ja|国頭村|12=draft|12=draft}}). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful {{ill|Daimyō|ja|大名|12=draft|12=draft}} family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|12=draft|12=draft}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|12=draft|12=draft}} (presently part of {{ill|Nanjo City|ja|南城市|12=draft|12=draft}} (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|12=draft|12=draft}}. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|12=draft|12=draft}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|12=draft|12=draft}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a {{ill|Bachi|ja|撥|12=draft|12=draft}}, and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|12=draft|12=draft}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|12=draft|12=draft}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|12=draft|12=draft}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|12=draft|12=draft}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|12=draft|12=draft}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in {{ill|Shuri|ja|首里|12=draft|12=draft}} in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with {{ill|Hansen's Disease|ja|ハンセン病|12=draft|12=draft}}, so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the {{ill|bone washing|ja|洗骨|12=draft|12=draft}} took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|12=draft|12=draft}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|12=draft|12=draft}} style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the {{ill|Gosamaru|ja|護佐丸|12=draft|12=draft}} grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|12=draft|12=draft}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦|12=draft|12=draft}}, with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.

The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|12=draft|12=draft}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|12=draft|12=draft}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|12=draft|12=draft}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|12=draft|12=draft}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|12=draft|12=draft}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in {{ill|Shuri|ja|首里|12=draft|12=draft}} in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with {{ill|Hansen's Disease|ja|ハンセン病|12=draft|12=draft}}, so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the {{ill|bone washing|ja|洗骨|12=draft|12=draft}} took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|12=draft|12=draft}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|12=draft|12=draft}} style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the {{ill|Gosamaru|ja|護佐丸|12=draft|12=draft}} grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|12=draft|12=draft}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦|12=draft|12=draft}}, with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.
==See Also== * [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]] * [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]]
==External Links== * [http://www.city.urasoe.lg.jp/article.php/siko00193 'I Want to Visit the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun' (Urasoe City Homepage)]

{{draft categories| [[Category:Mukai]] [[Category:Okinawan Tombs]] [[Category:Municipality-designated Historic Sites in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Urasoe City]] [[Category:Ryukyu Shinto]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }}
[[sn:Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave]] [[ja:玉城朝薫の墓]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave]][[Category:qq]]

{{One source|date=February 9, 2014 (Sun) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|ja|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] '''Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave''' (玉城朝薫の墓) is the tomb of the progenitor of {{ill|Kumiodori|ja|組踊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]], located in the {{ill|Maeda (Urasoe)|ja|前田 (浦添市)|lt=Maeda|lt_ja=前田|qq=draft|qq=draft}} area of {{ill|Urasoe City|ja|浦添市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a {{ill|Historic Site|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|3=史跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by Urasoe City.
==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of {{ill|Kunigami Village|ja|国頭村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful {{ill|Daimyō|ja|大名|qq=draft|qq=draft}} family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (presently part of {{ill|Nanjo City|ja|南城市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a {{ill|Bachi|ja|撥|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in {{ill|Shuri|ja|首里|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with {{ill|Hansen's Disease|ja|ハンセン病|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the {{ill|bone washing|ja|洗骨|qq=draft|qq=draft}} took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the {{ill|Gosamaru|ja|護佐丸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.

revision.comment: 13 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:玉城朝薫の墓 up to rev 96619352
revision.text:
{{単一の出典|date=2014年2月9日 (日) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）]] '''玉城朝薫の墓'''（たまぐすくちょうくんのはか）は、[[沖縄県]][[浦添市]][[前田 (浦添市)|前田]]にある[[組踊]]の祖・[[玉城朝薫|玉城親方朝薫]]の墓。墓様式は[[亀甲墓]]の祖型をなす独特の形をしている。浦添市指定[[史跡]]。
==概要== この墓は正確には玉城朝薫個人の墓ではなく、朝薫を含む[[向氏辺土名殿内]]の歴代墓（家族墓）である。辺土名殿内は、琉球王国末期には国頭[[間切]]（現・[[国頭村]]）辺土名村の脇地頭（一村の領主）を務めたが、玉城朝薫存命の頃は、玉城間切（現：[[南城市]]（玉城地区）） の[[総地頭]]職の地位にある[[大名]]家であった。
墓の概観は、亀甲墓の祖型をなすといわれる独特な形をしている。屋根は戦災で消失したが、戦前の写真から亀甲形ではなく、[[羽地朝秀の墓]]同様、[[かまぼこ]]形をしていたことが明らかとなっている。墓室正面の左右に拡がる袖石（に相当する石垣）は、亀甲墓のような屏風状に直線が折れ曲がる形式ではなく、緩やかな弧状を描きながら左右に拡がっている。墓口前には、幅48センチメートルのサンミデー（供物台）があり、墓口から約5メートル先には、墓庭を区切る縁石が外へ少し張り出しながら弧状に敷かれており、袖石とこの縁石に囲まれた墓庭部は[[撥]]（ばち）のような形をしている。墓庭は縁石部から外側より一段高く形成されている。墓庭の更に先には石段があったと推定されるが、戦災で消失している。袖石の外側には一段低くなった石垣が少し続いているが、これも戦災で失われたのか、途中で左右非対称に途切れている。墓庭の外には石碑の台座が残されているが、碑身は戦災で失われている。
墓室内部は、幅2.39メートル、奥行き2.73メートル、面積6.52平方メートルで、およそ4畳半の広さである。室内の壁は相方積みによる石壁（石材は[[琉球石灰岩]]）である。[[宜野湾御殿の墓]]のように漆喰で化粧はされていない。天井の高さは約1.8メートルで、天井を支えるために四本の石柱が墓室内に設置されている。このタイプは「[[摩文仁家の墓]]（[[摩文仁御殿]]の墓）」と同様で、珍しい構造である。石柱頭には二本の石梁を渡し、天井石版を支えている。天井石版は「ニービの骨（細粒砂岩）」製である。墓室正面奥には幅62センチメートル、高さ70センチメートルの石棚があり、玉城朝薫の[[厨子甕]]（[[蔵骨器]]）もそこに置かれていた。この石棚の更に奥に幅78×奥行き70センチメートルの小さな玄室があり、その中には遺骨がそのまま葬られている。
墓室内から厨子甕は、合計24、5基確認されているが、戦災で墓室の一部が破壊され、そこから土砂が流れ込んだために破損した厨子甕もあり、正確な総数は不明である。内訳は、石製家型厨子が一つ、他はすべて陶製厨子甕である。陶製はすべて甕型で御殿型（家型）は一つも含まれていない。玉城朝薫の厨子甕はマンガン掛け厨子甕である。朝薫の厨子甕も含め、かつて総地頭家であった名家にしては全体に質素である。他に墓室内から墓中符が一つ見つかっているが、造墓年などは書かれていない。 [[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|玉城朝薫旧墓「一ツ墓」]] 玉城朝薫は[[首里]]石嶺町にあった、俗に「一ツ（一人）墓」と呼ばれる個人墓に葬られていた。1899（明治32）年に前田の墓の厨子甕整理が行われたらしく、その際に石嶺の墓から朝薫の厨子甕が移葬されたと推測されている。移葬後、一ツ墓は空き墓となったが昭和初期まではまだ残っていた。王国時代、個人墓は[[ハンセン病]]患者など特殊な事例に限られており、朝薫が個人墓に葬られたのは当時としては異様な出来事であるが、なにゆえそうなったのかは不明である。また、厨子甕の銘書から[[洗骨]]が死後71年後の1805（[[嘉慶 (清)|嘉慶]]10）年であったことが判明しているが、これも通常の洗骨年（3~7年）から考えると異例である。
玉城朝薫の墓は、亀甲墓の祖型をなす墓と位置づけられているが、造墓年は[[護佐丸]]の墓（1686年）や[[伊江御殿墓]]（1687年）より以前であったのか、正確には分かっていない。[[沖縄戦]]で墓本体の上半分が吹き飛ぶなど、大きな被害を受けた。1988（昭和63）年に本格的な発掘調査が行われ、1995（平成7）年に浦添市の史跡に指定された。2005（平成17）年、修復工事が行われ往時の姿によみがえった。
==参考文献== *浦添市教育委員会編『玉城朝薫の墓調査報告書』 浦添市教育委員会 1989年
==関連項目== *[[亀甲墓]] *[[玉城朝薫]]
==外部リンク== *[http://www.city.urasoe.lg.jp/article.php/siko00193 「玉城朝薫の墓をたずねたい」（浦添市ホームページ）]

{{単一の出典|date=2014年2月9日 (日) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）]] '''玉城朝薫の墓'''（たまぐすくちょうくんのはか）は、[[沖縄県]][[浦添市]][[前田 (浦添市)|前田]]にある[[組踊]]の祖・[[玉城朝薫|玉城親方朝薫]]の墓。墓様式は[[亀甲墓]]の祖型をなす独特の形をしている。浦添市指定[[史跡]]。

墓の概観は、亀甲墓の祖型をなすといわれる独特な形をしている。屋根は戦災で消失したが、戦前の写真から亀甲形ではなく、[[羽地朝秀の墓]]同様、[[かまぼこ]]形をしていたことが明らかとなっている。墓室正面の左右に拡がる袖石（に相当する石垣）は、亀甲墓のような屏風状に直線が折れ曲がる形式ではなく、緩やかな弧状を描きながら左右に拡がっている。墓口前には、幅48センチメートルのサンミデー（供物台）があり、墓口から約5メートル先には、墓庭を区切る縁石が外へ少し張り出しながら弧状に敷かれており、袖石とこの縁石に囲まれた墓庭部は[[撥]]（ばち）のような形をしている。墓庭は縁石部から外側より一段高く形成されている。墓庭の更に先には石段があったと推定されるが、戦災で消失している。袖石の外側には一段低くなった石垣が少し続いているが、これも戦災で失われたのか、途中で左右非対称に途切れている。墓庭の外には石碑の台座が残されているが、碑身は戦災で失われている。
墓室内部は、幅2.39メートル、奥行き2.73メートル、面積6.52平方メートルで、およそ4畳半の広さである。室内の壁は相方積みによる石壁（石材は[[琉球石灰岩]]）である。[[宜野湾御殿の墓]]のように漆喰で化粧はされていない。天井の高さは約1.8メートルで、天井を支えるために四本の石柱が墓室内に設置されている。このタイプは「[[摩文仁家の墓]]（[[摩文仁御殿]]の墓）」と同様で、珍しい構造である。石柱頭には二本の石梁を渡し、天井石版を支えている。天井石版は「ニービの骨（細粒砂岩）」製である。墓室正面奥には幅62センチメートル、高さ70センチメートルの石棚があり、玉城朝薫の[[厨子甕]]（[[蔵骨器]]）もそこに置かれていた。この石棚の更に奥に幅78×奥行き70センチメートルの小さな玄室があり、その中には遺骨がそのまま葬られている。
墓室内から厨子甕は、合計24、5基確認されているが、戦災で墓室の一部が破壊され、そこから土砂が流れ込んだために破損した厨子甕もあり、正確な総数は不明である。内訳は、石製家型厨子が一つ、他はすべて陶製厨子甕である。陶製はすべて甕型で御殿型（家型）は一つも含まれていない。玉城朝薫の厨子甕はマンガン掛け厨子甕である。朝薫の厨子甕も含め、かつて総地頭家であった名家にしては全体に質素である。他に墓室内から墓中符が一つ見つかっているが、造墓年などは書かれていない。 [[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|玉城朝薫旧墓「一ツ墓」]] 玉城朝薫は[[首里]]石嶺町にあった、俗に「一ツ（一人）墓」と呼ばれる個人墓に葬られていた。1899（明治32）年に前田の墓の厨子甕整理が行われたらしく、その際に石嶺の墓から朝薫の厨子甕が移葬されたと推測されている。移葬後、一ツ墓は空き墓となったが昭和初期まではまだ残っていた。王国時代、個人墓は[[ハンセン病]]患者など特殊な事例に限られており、朝薫が個人墓に葬られたのは当時としては異様な出来事であるが、なにゆえそうなったのかは不明である。また、厨子甕の銘書から[[洗骨]]が死後71年後の1805（[[嘉慶 (清)|嘉慶]]10）年であったことが判明しているが、これも通常の洗骨年（3~7年）から考えると異例である。
玉城朝薫の墓は、亀甲墓の祖型をなす墓と位置づけられているが、造墓年は[[護佐丸]]の墓（1686年）や[[伊江御殿墓]]（1687年）より以前であったのか、正確には分かっていない。[[沖縄戦]]で墓本体の上半分が吹き飛ぶなど、大きな被害を受けた。1988（昭和63）年に本格的な発掘調査が行われ、1995（平成7）年に浦添市の史跡に指定された。2005（平成17）年、修復工事が行われ往時の姿によみがえった。
==参考文献== *浦添市教育委員会編『玉城朝薫の墓調査報告書』 浦添市教育委員会 1989年

==外部リンク== *[http://www.city.urasoe.lg.jp/article.php/siko00193 「玉城朝薫の墓をたずねたい」（浦添市ホームページ）]
{{coord|26|14|16.8|N|127|43|47.0|E|region:JP-47|display=title}} {{デフォルトソート:たまくすくちようくんのはか}} [[Category:向氏]] [[Category:沖縄県の墓陵]] [[Category:沖縄県内の市町村指定史跡]] [[Category:浦添市の建築物]]

{{One source|date=February 9, 2014 (Sun) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|ja|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] '''Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave''' (玉城朝薫の墓) is the tomb of the progenitor of {{ill|Kumiodori|ja|組踊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]], located in the {{ill|Maeda (Urasoe)|ja|前田 (浦添市)|lt=Maeda|lt_ja=前田|qq=draft|qq=draft}} area of {{ill|Urasoe City|ja|浦添市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a {{ill|Historic Site|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|3=史跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by Urasoe City.
==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of {{ill|Kunigami Village|ja|国頭村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful {{ill|Daimyō|ja|大名|qq=draft|qq=draft}} family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (presently part of {{ill|Nanjo City|ja|南城市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a {{ill|Bachi|ja|撥|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in {{ill|Shuri|ja|首里|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with {{ill|Hansen's Disease|ja|ハンセン病|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the {{ill|bone washing|ja|洗骨|qq=draft|qq=draft}} took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the {{ill|Gosamaru|ja|護佐丸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.

The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a {{ill|Bachi|ja|撥|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in {{ill|Shuri|ja|首里|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with {{ill|Hansen's Disease|ja|ハンセン病|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the {{ill|bone washing|ja|洗骨|qq=draft|qq=draft}} took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the {{ill|Gosamaru|ja|護佐丸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.
==See Also== * [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]] * [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]]

[[sn:Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave]] [[ja:玉城朝薫の墓]]

{{One source|date=February 9, 2014 (Sun) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|ja|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] '''Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave''' (玉城朝薫の墓) is the tomb of the progenitor of {{ill|Kumiodori|ja|組踊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]], located in the {{ill|Maeda (Urasoe)|ja|前田 (浦添市)|lt=Maeda|lt_ja=前田|qq=draft|qq=draft}} area of {{ill|Urasoe City|ja|浦添市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a {{ill|Historic Site|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|3=史跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by Urasoe City.
==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of {{ill|Kunigami Village|ja|国頭村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful {{ill|Daimyō|ja|大名|qq=draft|qq=draft}} family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (presently part of {{ill|Nanjo City|ja|南城市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a {{ill|Bachi|ja|撥|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in {{ill|Shuri|ja|首里|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with {{ill|Hansen's Disease|ja|ハンセン病|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the {{ill|bone washing|ja|洗骨|qq=draft|qq=draft}} took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the {{ill|Gosamaru|ja|護佐丸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.

==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukai Clan Hendo-na|qq=}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makigiri|qq=}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of {{ill|Kunigami Village|ja|国頭村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunigami Village|qq=}}). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful {{ill|Daimyō|ja|大名|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimyō|qq=}} family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjitō|qq=}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Makigiri|qq=}} (presently part of {{ill|Nanjo City|ja|南城市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanjo City|qq=}} (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Turtle Shell Tomb|qq=}}. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaboko shape|qq=}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji Chōshū's Grave|qq=}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a {{ill|Bachi|ja|撥|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bachi|qq=}}, and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Limestone|qq=}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginowan Palace Grave|qq=}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in {{ill|Shuri|ja|首里|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuri|qq=}} in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with {{ill|Hansen's Disease|ja|ハンセン病|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hansen's Disease|qq=}}, so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the {{ill|bone washing|ja|洗骨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bone washing|qq=}} took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Turtle Shell Tomb|qq=}} style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the {{ill|Gosamaru|ja|護佐丸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosamaru|qq=}} grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|ja|沖縄戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Okinawa|qq=}}, with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.
==See Also== * [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]] * [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]]
==External Links== * [http://www.city.urasoe.lg.jp/article.php/siko00193 'I Want to Visit the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun' (Urasoe City Homepage)]

The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a {{ill|Historic Site|ja|史跡|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|3=史跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic Site|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic site|qq=}} by Urasoe City.

However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful {{ill|Daimyō|ja|大名|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimyō|qq=}} family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjitō|qq=}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Makigiri|qq=}} (presently part of {{ill|Nanjo City|ja|南城市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanjo City|qq=}} (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the {{ill|Turtle Shell Tomb|ja|亀甲墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Turtle Shell Tomb|qq=}}. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Limestone|qq=}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginowan Palace Grave|qq=}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.

{{translated page|ja|玉城朝薫の墓|version=96619352|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Municipality-designated Historic Sites in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Urasoe City]] [[Category:Ryukyu Shinto]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }}
[[sn:Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave]] [[ja:玉城朝薫の墓]]

{{One source|date=February 9, 2014 (Sun) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|ja|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|qq=}}]] '''Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave''' (玉城朝薫の墓) is the tomb of the progenitor of [[:en:Kumi Odori|Kumiodori]], [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]], located in the {{ill|Maeda (Urasoe)|ja|前田 (浦添市)|lt=Maeda|lt_ja=前田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maeda (Urasoe)|qq=}} area of [[:en:Urasoe, Okinawa|Urasoe City]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]] by Urasoe City.
==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukai Clan Hendo-na|qq=}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makigiri|qq=}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of [[:en:Kunigami, Okinawa|Kunigami Village]]). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful [[:en:Daimyo|Daimyō]] family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjitō|qq=}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Makigiri|qq=}} (presently part of [[:en:Nanjō|Nanjo City]] (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]]. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaboko shape|qq=}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji Chōshū's Grave|qq=}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a [[:en:Bachi|Bachi]], and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Limestone|qq=}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginowan Palace Grave|qq=}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.
==See Also== * [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]] * [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]]
==External Links== * [http://www.city.urasoe.lg.jp/article.php/siko00193 'I Want to Visit the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun' (Urasoe City Homepage)]

The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.
==See Also== * [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]] * [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]]
