revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Buildings and structures in Urasoe, Okinawa
revision.text:
{{One source|date=February 9, 2014 (Sun) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|ja|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|qq=}}]] '''Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave''' (玉城朝薫の墓) is the tomb of the progenitor of [[:en:Kumi Odori|Kumiodori]], [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]], located in the {{ill|Maeda (Urasoe)|ja|前田 (浦添市)|lt=Maeda|lt_ja=前田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maeda (Urasoe)|qq=}} area of [[:en:Urasoe, Okinawa|Urasoe City]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]] by Urasoe City.
==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukai Clan Hendo-na|qq=}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makigiri|qq=}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of [[:en:Kunigami, Okinawa|Kunigami Village]]). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful [[:en:Daimyo|Daimyō]] family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjitō|qq=}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Makigiri|qq=}} (presently part of [[:en:Nanjō|Nanjo City]] (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]]. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaboko shape|qq=}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji Chōshū's Grave|qq=}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a [[:en:Bachi|Bachi]], and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Limestone|qq=}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginowan Palace Grave|qq=}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.
==See Also== * [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]] * [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]]
==External Links== * [http://www.city.urasoe.lg.jp/article.php/siko00193 'I Want to Visit the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun' (Urasoe City Homepage)]

Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.

[[sn:Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave]] [[ja:玉城朝薫の墓]] [[Category:向氏]] [[Category:Graves of Okinawa]] [[Category:沖縄県内の市町村指定史跡]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Urasoe, Okinawa]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

{{One source|date=February 9, 2014 (Sun) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|ja|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] '''Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave''' (玉城朝薫の墓) is the tomb of the progenitor of [[:en:Kumi Odori|Kumiodori]], [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]], located in the {{ill|Maeda (Urasoe)|ja|前田 (浦添市)|lt=Maeda|lt_ja=前田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maeda (Urasoe)|qq=}} area of [[:en:Urasoe, Okinawa|Urasoe City]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]] by Urasoe City.
==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukai Clan Hendo-na|qq=}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makigiri|qq=}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of [[:en:Kunigami, Okinawa|Kunigami Village]]). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful [[:en:Daimyo|Daimyō]] family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjitō|qq=}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Makigiri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (presently part of [[:en:Nanjō|Nanjo City]] (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]]. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaboko shape|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蒲鉾}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji Chōshū's Grave|qq=}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a [[:en:Bachi|Bachi]], and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Limestone|qq=}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginowan Palace Grave|qq=}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.

Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaboko shape|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蒲鉾}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji Chōshū's Grave|qq=}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a [[:en:Bachi|Bachi]], and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Limestone|qq=}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginowan Palace Grave|qq=}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.
==See Also== * [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]] * [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]]
==External Links== * [http://www.city.urasoe.lg.jp/article.php/siko00193 'I Want to Visit the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun' (Urasoe City Homepage)]

{{One source|date=February 9, 2014 (Sun) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|ja|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] '''Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave''' (玉城朝薫の墓) is the tomb of the progenitor of [[:en:Kumi Odori|Kumiodori]], [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]], located in the {{ill|Maeda (Urasoe)|ja|前田 (浦添市)|lt=Maeda|lt_ja=前田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maeda (Urasoe)|qq=}} area of [[:en:Urasoe, Okinawa|Urasoe City]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]] by Urasoe City.
==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukai Clan Hendo-na|qq=}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makigiri|qq=}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of [[:en:Kunigami, Okinawa|Kunigami Village]]). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful [[:en:Daimyo|Daimyō]] family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjitō|qq=}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Makigiri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (presently part of [[:en:Nanjō|Nanjo City]] (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]]. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaboko shape|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蒲鉾}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji Chōshū's Grave|qq=}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a [[:en:Bachi|Bachi]], and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Limestone|qq=}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginowan Palace Grave|qq=}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.

However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.

The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]] by Urasoe City.
==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukai Clan Hendo-na|qq=}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makigiri|qq=}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of [[:en:Kunigami, Okinawa|Kunigami Village]]). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful [[:en:Daimyo|Daimyō]] family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjitō|qq=}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Makigiri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (presently part of [[:en:Nanjō|Nanjo City]] (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]]. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaboko shape|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蒲鉾}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji Chōshū's Grave|qq=}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a [[:en:Bachi|Bachi]], and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Limestone|qq=}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginowan Palace Grave|qq=}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.
==See Also== * [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]] * [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]]
==External Links== * [http://www.city.urasoe.lg.jp/article.php/siko00193 'I Want to Visit the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun' (Urasoe City Homepage)]

This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=}}), a rare structure. At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=|1=Ie Udun tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379638}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.

{{draft categories|
[[Category:Completion tier 1]] }}
[[sn:Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave]] [[ja:玉城朝薫の墓]] [[Category:Mukō Clan]] [[Category:Graves of Okinawa]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Urasoe, Okinawa]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11572474]] Wikidata name: Tomb of Tamagusuku Chōkun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{One source|date=February 9, 2014 (Sun) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|ja|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] '''Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave''' (玉城朝薫の墓) is the tomb of the progenitor of [[:en:Kumi Odori|Kumiodori]], [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]], located in the {{ill|Maeda (Urasoe)|ja|前田 (浦添市)|lt=Maeda|lt_ja=前田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maeda (Urasoe)|qq=|1=Maeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11397361}} area of [[:en:Urasoe, Okinawa|Urasoe City]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]] by Urasoe City.
==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukai Clan Hendo-na|qq=|1=Shō-uji Hentona Dunchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11416007}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makigiri|qq=|1=magiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2113743}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of [[:en:Kunigami, Okinawa|Kunigami Village]]). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful [[:en:Daimyo|Daimyō]] family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjitō|qq=|1=sō-jitō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11607640}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Makigiri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (presently part of [[:en:Nanjō|Nanjo City]] (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]]. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaboko shape|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蒲鉾|1=kamaboko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q166204}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji Chōshū's Grave|qq=|1=Tomb of Haneji Chōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610069}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a [[:en:Bachi|Bachi]], and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Limestone|qq=|1=Ryukyuan limestone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573686}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginowan Palace Grave|qq=|1=Ginowan Udun tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452305}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=|1=Mabuni Family Tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498615}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=|1=Mabuni Udun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498616}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=|1=zushigame|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410102}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=|1=Bone Urn|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24886959}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=|1=Jiaqing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1077543}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=|1=Ie Udun tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379638}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.

The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]] by Urasoe City.

The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]]. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaboko shape|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蒲鉾|1=kamaboko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q166204}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji Chōshū's Grave|qq=|1=Tomb of Haneji Chōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610069}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a [[:en:Bachi|Bachi]], and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Limestone|qq=|1=Ryukyuan limestone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573686}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginowan Palace Grave|qq=|1=Ginowan Udun tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452305}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=|1=Mabuni Family Tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498615}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=|1=Mabuni Udun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498616}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=|1=zushigame|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410102}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=|1=Bone Urn|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24886959}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.

Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=|1=Jiaqing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1077543}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=|1=Ie Udun tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379638}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.

The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]] by Urasoe City.

However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful [[:en:Daimyo|Daimyō]] family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjitō|qq=|1=sō-jitō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11607640}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Makigiri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (presently part of [[:en:Nanjō|Nanjo City]] (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]]. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaboko shape|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蒲鉾|1=kamaboko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q166204}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji Chōshū's Grave|qq=|1=Tomb of Haneji Chōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610069}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a [[:en:Bachi|Bachi]], and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Limestone|qq=|1=Ryukyuan limestone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573686}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginowan Palace Grave|qq=|1=Ginowan Udun tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452305}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=|1=Mabuni Family Tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498615}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=|1=Mabuni Udun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498616}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=|1=zushigame|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410102}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=|1=Bone Urn|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24886959}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=|1=Jiaqing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1077543}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=|1=Ie Udun tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379638}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:玉城朝薫の墓]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11572474]] Wikidata name: Tomb of Tamagusuku Chōkun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{One source|date=February 9, 2014 (Sun) 07:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun.JPG|thumb|300px|{{ill|Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|ja|玉城朝薫の墓（浦添市前田）|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] '''Tamagusuku Chokun's Grave''' (玉城朝薫の墓) is the tomb of the progenitor of [[:en:Kumi Odori|Kumiodori]], [[:en:Tamagusuku Chōkun|Tamagusuku Chōkun]], located in the {{ill|Maeda (Urasoe)|ja|前田 (浦添市)|lt=Maeda|lt_ja=前田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maeda (Urasoe)|qq=|1=Maeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11397361}} area of [[:en:Urasoe, Okinawa|Urasoe City]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. The tomb’s style is of a unique form that constitutes the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtleback tomb]], and it is designated as a [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]] by Urasoe City.
==Overview== This tomb is not strictly the individual grave of Tamagusuku Chokun; rather, it is the family tomb of the successive generations of the {{ill|Mukai Clan Hendo-na|ja|向氏辺土名殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukai Clan Hendo-na|qq=|1=Shō-uji Hentona Dunchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11416007}}, which includes Chokun. In the late period of the Ryukyu Kingdom, the Hendo-na served as the local chieftain (waki-jitō) of Hendo-na Village in Kunigami under {{ill|Makigiri|ja|間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makigiri|qq=|1=magiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2113743}}, who presided over Hendo-na in the village of Kunigami (presently part of [[:en:Kunigami, Okinawa|Kunigami Village]]). However, during Chokun’s lifetime, the Tamagusuku family was a powerful [[:en:Daimyo|Daimyō]] family holding the position of {{ill|Sōjitō|ja|総地頭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōjitō|qq=|1=sō-jitō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11607640}} under {{ill|Tamagusuku Makigiri|ja|玉城間切|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamagusuku Makigiri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (presently part of [[:en:Nanjō|Nanjo City]] (Tamagusuku area)).
The overall structure of the tomb is said to be a unique form representing the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]]. Although the roof was lost due to war damage, pre-war photographs reveal that rather than being in a turtle shell shape, it was in a {{ill|Kamaboko shape|ja|かまぼこ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaboko shape|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蒲鉾|1=kamaboko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q166204}}, similar to the {{ill|Haji Chōshū's Grave|ja|羽地朝秀の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji Chōshū's Grave|qq=|1=Tomb of Haneji Chōshū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610069}}. The side stones extending to the left and right of the tomb chamber’s front—functioning as “sode ishi”—are not arranged in the sharply folded, screen-like manner seen in typical turtle shell tombs; instead, they extend outward in a gentle arc. In front of the tomb entrance, there is an offering table (sanmidē) 48 centimeters wide, and approximately 5 meters from the entrance a border stone is laid in an arched form that slightly projects outward. The tombyard enclosed by the side stones and this border stone is shaped like a [[:en:Bachi|Bachi]], and it is constructed one step higher than the area outside the border. It is presumed that there were stone steps beyond the tombyard, but these have been lost to war damage. A slightly lower stone wall continues just outside the side stones, though it too has either been destroyed by the war or becomes asymmetrical midway. Outside the tombyard, the base of a stone monument remains, although the monument’s stone tablet was lost during the conflict.
The interior of the tomb chamber measures 2.39 meters in width, 2.73 meters in depth, and covers an area of 6.52 square meters—roughly equivalent to 4½ tatami mats. The walls inside are constructed in an aikatazumi style using stone (the material being {{ill|Ryukyu Limestone|ja|琉球石灰岩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryukyu Limestone|qq=|1=Ryukyuan limestone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573686}}) and are not finished with plaster, unlike the {{ill|Ginowan Palace Grave|ja|宜野湾御殿の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginowan Palace Grave|qq=|1=Ginowan Udun tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452305}}. The ceiling, at about 1.8 meters high, is supported by four stone pillars installed within the chamber. This type is similar to that of the {{ill|Mabunjin Family Grave|ja|摩文仁家の墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Family Grave|qq=|1=Mabuni Family Tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498615}} (the grave of the {{ill|Mabunjin Palace|ja|摩文仁御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabunjin Palace|qq=|1=Mabuni Udun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11498616}}), a rare structure. Two stone beams span the tops of the pillars to support the ceiling slab, which is made of “Niibi no Hone” (fine-grained sandstone). At the back of the chamber’s front, there is a stone shelf measuring 62 centimeters in width and 70 centimeters in height, on which was placed the {{ill|Chushi Urn|ja|厨子甕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chushi Urn|qq=|1=zushigame|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410102}} ({{ill|Bone Urn|ja|蔵骨器|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bone Urn|qq=|1=Bone Urn|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24886959}}) of Tamagusuku Chokun. Beyond this shelf lies a small antechamber, 78 centimeters wide by 70 centimeters deep, in which the remains were interred in situ.
Within the tomb chamber, a total of 24 or 25 chushi urns have been identified. However, due to war damage that partially destroyed the chamber and allowed debris to flow in—damaging some of the urns—the exact total is uncertain. Of these, one is a stone-made family-type chushi urn, while all the others are ceramic chushi urns; notably, every ceramic urn is of the urn type, with no palace (family) type included. The chushi urn of Tamagusuku Chokun is adorned with manganese. Including his, the urns of this once-prominent Sōjitō family are remarkably modest. Additionally, a tomb marker (chūfu) was found within the chamber, though it bears no inscription regarding the year of construction.
[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|qq=|1=Jiaqing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1077543}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|qq=|1=Ie Udun tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379638}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.

[[File:Tamagusuku Chokun old tomb.jpg|thumb|Tamagusuku Chokun's former grave, "Hitotsu Tomb"]] Tamagusuku Chokun was interred in an individual grave—commonly known as the “Hitotsu (solitary) Tomb”—located in [[:en:Shuri, Okinawa|Shuri]] in the Ishimine area. It is presumed that in 1899 (Meiji 32), during a reorganization of the chushi urns in the Maeda tomb, Chokun’s chushi urn was relocated from the tomb in Ishimine. After the transfer, the Hitotsu Tomb became vacant, though it remained intact until the early Shōwa period. In the Kingdom period, individual graves were reserved for exceptional cases such as patients with [[:en:Leprosy|Hansen's Disease]], so Chokun’s burial in an individual grave was unusual; the reason for this remains unknown. Furthermore, an inscription on the chushi urn indicates that the [[:en:Bone collecting|bone washing]] took place 71 years after death, in 1805 (the 10th year of {{ill|Kakēi (Qing)|ja|嘉慶 (清)|lt=Kakēi|lt_ja=嘉慶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kakēi (Qing)|1=Jiaqing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1077543}}), which is also atypical compared to the usual bone washing period of 3 to 7 years.
Tamagusuku Chokun's grave is regarded as the prototype of the [[:en:Turtleback tomb|Turtle Shell Tomb]] style. However, it remains unclear whether its construction predates that of the [[:en:Gosamaru|Gosamaru]] grave (1686) or the {{ill|Iei Palace Grave|ja|伊江御殿墓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iei Palace Grave|1=Ie Udun tomb|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379638}} (1687). The tomb suffered significant damage during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]], with the upper half of the main structure being blown off. In 1988 (Shōwa 63), a full-scale excavation was conducted, and in 1995 (Heisei 7) it was designated as a Historic Site by Urasoe City. Restoration work in 2005 (Heisei 17) revived the tomb to its former appearance.
==References== * Urasoe City Board of Education (ed.), ''Investigation Report on the Grave of Tamagusuku Chokun'', Urasoe City Board of Education, 1989.
